quiz_questions.question_text: Do all cells in your body contain the same DNA?

options.text: Only brain cells have DNA
Yes — nearly all body cells have the same complete genome
No — each cell type has different DNA
Cells get new DNA each time they divide

quiz_questions.explanation: Nearly all cells in your body contain an identical copy of your complete genome — about 20,000 genes. What makes a muscle cell different from a nerve cell is which genes are turned on or off, not which genes are present.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the main role of a transcription factor?

options.text: It copies DNA during replication
It transports mRNA out of the nucleus
It builds polypeptides at the ribosome
It binds to DNA and helps control whether a gene is transcribed

quiz_questions.explanation: Transcription factors are proteins that bind to specific DNA sequences near a gene's promoter. They can activate or repress transcription by recruiting or blocking RNA polymerase.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the lac operon, what happens when lactose is present?

options.text: The repressor binds more tightly to the operator
The promoter is methylated
The structural genes are deleted
The repressor releases the operator, allowing transcription

quiz_questions.explanation: When lactose is present, it is converted to allolactose, which binds the lac repressor and changes its shape. The repressor can no longer attach to the operator, so RNA polymerase proceeds and the lactose-digesting enzymes are produced.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does histone acetylation generally turn genes ON or OFF?

options.text: It deletes the gene permanently
OFF — acetylation compresses chromatin tighter
ON — acetylation loosens DNA from histones, making genes accessible
It has no effect on gene expression

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding acetyl groups to histone tails neutralizes their positive charge, weakening the attraction to negatively charged DNA. The chromatin opens up (euchromatin), allowing transcription factors and RNA polymerase to access the gene.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does DNA methylation typically do to a gene?

options.text: Removes the gene from the chromosome
Changes the DNA sequence
Silences it — methyl groups block transcription factor binding
Activates it — methylation promotes transcription

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA methylation (adding -CH₃ groups to cytosine bases at CpG sites) usually silences genes by preventing transcription factors from binding the promoter region.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between an oncogene and a tumor suppressor?

options.text: They are the same thing
Tumor suppressors cause cancer; oncogenes prevent it
Both promote uncontrolled cell growth
Oncogenes promote cell growth; tumor suppressors stop cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: Oncogenes are mutated versions of normal growth-promoting genes (proto-oncogenes) — they act like a gas pedal stuck down. Tumor suppressors normally stop or slow the cell cycle — they act as brakes. Cancer usually involves both accelerators stuck on AND brakes disabled.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is alternative splicing?

options.text: Different combinations of exons produce different mRNAs from one gene
Ribosomes skip certain codons during translation
A gene is duplicated multiple times
DNA is cut into pieces and rearranged

quiz_questions.explanation: Alternative splicing is a post-transcriptional process where different exon combinations are included in the mature mRNA. This allows one gene to code for multiple protein variants — a key reason humans can have ~100,000 protein types from only ~20,000 genes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the function of the p53 protein?

options.text: It pauses the cell cycle when DNA damage is detected and can trigger cell death
It digests proteins in the lysosome
It replicates DNA
It promotes rapid cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: p53 is called the 'guardian of the genome.' When it detects DNA damage, it halts the cell cycle to allow repair. If the damage is too severe, p53 triggers apoptosis (programmed cell death). It is mutated in over 50% of human cancers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are Hox genes?

options.text: Genes that code for hemoglobin
Genes only found in plants
Genes that cause cancer when mutated
Master control genes that determine body segment identity during development

quiz_questions.explanation: Hox (homeotic) genes are transcription factors that specify which body structures develop in each segment — head, thorax, abdomen, etc. They are remarkably conserved across animals from fruit flies to humans.

quiz_questions.question_text: At which level is gene expression most commonly regulated in eukaryotes?

options.text: DNA replication, particularly regarding energy in
Transcription — controlling whether mRNA is made in the first place
Protein folding only under the given assumptions
Cell division, particularly regarding while under

quiz_questions.explanation: While gene expression can be regulated at many levels (epigenetic, transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, post-translational), transcription initiation is the primary control point — it is most energy-efficient to prevent unwanted mRNA from being made at all.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'gene expression.'

options.text: The total number of genes in an organism
The process by which information in a gene is used to make a functional product like a protein
The movement of chromosomes during mitosis
The copying of DNA before cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: Gene expression is the overall process of gene → mRNA → protein (or functional RNA). It involves transcription (DNA to mRNA) and translation (mRNA to protein). Not all genes are expressed in every cell.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an operon?

options.text: A membrane-bound organelle
A cluster of genes under the control of a single promoter, found in prokaryotes
A type of ribosome in eukaryotic cells
An enzyme that unwinds DNA

quiz_questions.explanation: An operon is a unit of gene regulation in bacteria that contains a promoter, an operator, and one or more structural genes transcribed as a single mRNA. The lac operon and trp operon are classic examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'epigenetic' mean?

options.text: The creation of new genes through evolution
Changes in gene expression that do not alter the DNA sequence itself
A type of genetic disease
Permanent mutations in the DNA code

quiz_questions.explanation: Epigenetics (literally 'above genetics') refers to heritable changes in gene activity that occur without any change to the DNA sequence. Key mechanisms include DNA methylation and histone modification. These marks can be influenced by environment, diet, and age.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'cell differentiation.'

options.text: The process by which a cell becomes specialized in structure and function
The transfer of DNA between bacteria
The death of damaged cells
The division of one cell into two identical cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Cell differentiation is the process during development where unspecialized cells become specialized (neurons, muscle cells, blood cells, etc.). It is driven by differential gene expression — different genes being turned on and off — not by changes in DNA.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a promoter?

options.text: A protein that degrades mRNA
A DNA sequence where RNA polymerase binds to begin transcription
The end signal of a gene
A type of RNA that carries amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: A promoter is a specific DNA sequence located upstream of a gene that serves as the binding site for RNA polymerase and associated transcription factors. It determines when and how often a gene is transcribed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an enhancer in genetics?

options.text: A distant regulatory DNA element that increases transcription of a target gene
A type of mutation that makes a protein work better
An enzyme that does proofreading during translation
A chemical that speeds up DNA replication

quiz_questions.explanation: An enhancer is a regulatory DNA element that can be thousands of base pairs away from the gene it controls. When activator proteins bind to the enhancer, the DNA loops so the enhancer contacts the promoter, boosting transcription.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is microRNA (miRNA)?

options.text: DNA fragments left over from viral infections
The smallest complete genome found in bacteria
Small RNA molecules that regulate gene expression by blocking or degrading mRNA
mRNA that codes for very small proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small (~22 nucleotide) non-coding RNA molecules. They bind to complementary sequences on target mRNAs, either preventing translation or triggering mRNA degradation. This is a form of post-transcriptional gene regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'proto-oncogene' mean?

options.text: A viral gene inserted into human DNA
A gene found only in cancer cells
A gene that always causes cancer
A normal gene that promotes cell growth and can become an oncogene if mutated

quiz_questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes are normal, essential genes that encode proteins promoting cell growth and division. When mutated (by point mutations, gene amplification, or chromosomal translocation), they become oncogenes — driving uncontrolled cell proliferation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is RNA interference (RNAi)?

options.text: A mechanism where small RNA molecules silence genes by destroying target mRNA
A method of DNA sequencing
When RNA polymerase blocks other enzymes from working
When two RNA molecules combine to make a protein

quiz_questions.explanation: RNA interference (RNAi) is a natural defense mechanism in which double-stranded RNA is cut by the enzyme Dicer into small interfering RNAs (siRNAs). These siRNAs guide the RISC complex to find and destroy complementary mRNA, silencing the gene post-transcriptionally.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'euchromatin' and 'heterochromatin.'

options.text: Euchromatin is found only in bacteria; heterochromatin in eukaryotes
Heterochromatin is loosely packed; euchromatin is tightly packed
Euchromatin is loosely packed and actively transcribed; heterochromatin is tightly packed and silenced
They are the same type of chromatin

quiz_questions.explanation: Euchromatin ('true chromatin') is lightly packed, open chromatin where genes are accessible for transcription — associated with histone acetylation. Heterochromatin is densely packed, closed chromatin where genes are silenced — associated with histone deacetylation and DNA methylation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company develops a drug that inhibits histone deacetylases (HDACs) in cancer cells. HDACs normally remove acetyl groups from histones. If the drug successfully blocks HDACs, what would you predict happens to tumor suppressor genes that were epigenetically silenced?

options.text: No change — histone modifications cannot affect gene expression
They become reactivated — blocking deacetylation keeps histones acetylated and chromatin open
They become more silenced — the drug compacts chromatin further
The DNA sequence of the tumor suppressors mutates

quiz_questions.explanation: HDAC inhibitors prevent acetyl group removal from histones, keeping chromatin in an open (euchromatin) state. This reactivates genes that cancer cells had silenced via histone deacetylation — including tumor suppressors. Drugs like vorinostat (SAHA) are FDA-approved cancer treatments based on this epigenetic mechanism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Identical twins raised in different environments are studied at age 60. Twin A, who smoked for decades, has significantly different gene expression patterns compared to Twin B. Their DNA sequences are still identical. A researcher finds heavy methylation on certain genes in Twin A that are unmethylated in Twin B. This study BEST illustrates:

options.text: Natural selection acting on the twins differently
The twins were not actually identical
Genetic mutation — smoking changed Twin A's DNA sequence
Epigenetic changes — environmental factors altered gene expression without changing DNA sequence

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a classic example of epigenetics. Identical twins start with the same DNA, but environmental factors (diet, stress, smoking, toxins) alter methylation patterns over time. These epigenetic changes modify gene expression WITHOUT changing the DNA sequence — explaining why identical twins can develop different diseases as they age.

quiz_questions.question_text: E. coli bacteria are grown on a medium with both glucose and lactose. A student predicts that the lac operon will be fully active because lactose is present. However, the bacteria preferentially use glucose and the lac operon is barely transcribed. Why?

options.text: When glucose is available, cAMP levels are low and CAP cannot activate the operon — even though the repressor is removed by lactose
Glucose directly binds and inactivates the lac enzymes
The lac operon only works when no sugars are present
Lactose acts as a repressor when glucose is also present

quiz_questions.explanation: The lac operon has dual regulation: negative control (lac repressor, removed by lactose) and positive control (CAP protein, activated by high cAMP when glucose is absent). Even when lactose removes the repressor, the operon needs CAP for strong transcription. With glucose present, cAMP is low, CAP is inactive, and the operon remains at minimal transcription. This is catabolite repression — bacteria prioritize glucose.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studying fruit flies discovers a mutant where legs grow out of the head where antennae should be. This bizarre phenotype is caused by a mutation in a Hox gene called Antennapedia that is expressed in the wrong body segment. What does this tell us about Hox genes?

options.text: Hox genes are master switches — expressing one in the wrong segment builds the wrong body structure for that location
The mutation deleted the antenna genes entirely
Fruit flies don't normally have antennae
Hox genes only affect eye development in fruit flies

quiz_questions.explanation: The Antennapedia mutation is a famous homeotic transformation. The Antennapedia gene normally specifies 'build thorax/legs here,' but when misexpressed in the head segment, it overrides the antenna program and builds legs instead. Hox gene order on the chromosome mirrors body segment order (collinearity).

quiz_questions.question_text: A breast cancer patient's tumor is analyzed. Doctors find that the Ras proto-oncogene has a point mutation making it permanently active, AND both copies of the p53 tumor suppressor gene are inactivated. Why do most cancers require MULTIPLE mutations rather than just one?

options.text: Cancer is caused only by environmental factors, not mutations
A single mutation in any gene will always cause cancer immediately
Cells have multiple safeguards — both growth promoters must be stuck ON and brakes must be disabled to cause uncontrolled division
Only mutations in p53 matter — Ras mutations have no role in cancer

quiz_questions.explanation: Cancer is a multi-step process requiring 5-7 mutations. Cells have built-in redundancy: oncogene activation alone can be countered by functional tumor suppressors. It usually takes hits to BOTH systems — stuck accelerators AND broken brakes — for uncontrolled proliferation. This explains why cancer risk increases with age.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher introduces double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) matching the mRNA of a disease-causing gene into cells. The protein product drops by 90%. The mechanism involves Dicer cutting the dsRNA into small fragments that guide the RISC complex to the target mRNA. This technique is called:

options.text: Alternative splicing — rearranging exons in the pre-mRNA
PCR — amplifying the gene for further study
CRISPR gene editing — cutting and replacing the DNA sequence
RNA interference (RNAi) — siRNAs direct the RISC complex to degrade specific mRNAs

quiz_questions.explanation: RNA interference (RNAi), discovered by Fire and Mello (Nobel Prize 2006), is a post-transcriptional gene silencing mechanism. dsRNA is processed by Dicer into ~21-nucleotide siRNAs that load into RISC to find and destroy complementary mRNA. The FDA-approved drug patisiran uses this mechanism.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Dscam gene in fruit flies produces over 38,000 different protein variants from a single gene — each neuron expresses a unique combination. The pre-mRNA has 115 exons that can be combined differently. This mechanism is:

options.text: Alternative splicing — including different exon combinations creates diverse mRNAs from one gene
Horizontal gene transfer — neurons pick up different genes from nearby cells
Somatic mutation — each neuron randomly mutates the gene differently
Gene amplification — the gene was copied 38,000 times

quiz_questions.explanation: Dscam is the most extreme example of alternative splicing — 115 exons combined in 38,016 ways from a single gene. Each neuron expresses a unique subset for self-recognition during neural wiring. This illustrates why ~20,000 human genes can produce far more than 20,000 proteins.

quiz_questions.question_text: Estrogen passes through the cell membrane and binds an intracellular receptor. The estrogen-receptor complex enters the nucleus and directly binds DNA at estrogen response elements (EREs), activating target genes. At which level does estrogen regulate gene expression?

options.text: Post-translational — estrogen modifies completed proteins
Transcriptional — the hormone-receptor complex acts as a transcription factor directly activating genes
DNA replication — estrogen controls when chromosomes are copied
Post-transcriptional — estrogen edits mRNA after transcription

quiz_questions.explanation: Steroid hormones (estrogen, testosterone, cortisol) are lipid-soluble, so they cross cell membranes and bind intracellular receptors. The complex acts as a transcription factor — entering the nucleus and directly binding DNA to activate specific genes. This is transcriptional-level regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with Li-Fraumeni syndrome inherits one non-functional copy of p53. She has one working copy and one broken copy. Why doesn't the one working copy fully protect her from cancer?

options.text: One copy of p53 immediately causes cancer at birth
p53 doesn't actually prevent cancer
The broken copy produces a toxin that harms cells
If the remaining good copy is lost through a second mutation (loss of heterozygosity), she loses all p53 function

quiz_questions.explanation: This illustrates Knudson's two-hit hypothesis. Inheriting one mutant p53 allele means only one more 'hit' is needed to eliminate the gene. Over a normal lifetime (~10¹⁶ cell divisions), the probability of that second hit is very high — hence the ~90% cancer risk in Li-Fraumeni syndrome.

quiz_questions.question_text: A growth factor binds a cell surface receptor, triggering Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK. ERK enters the nucleus and activates transcription factors for growth genes. ~30% of all cancers have a permanently active Ras mutation. Why is Ras being constantly active so dangerous?

options.text: It causes cells to immediately die by apoptosis
Ras mutations are harmless and have no effect on cell growth
It sends continuous growth signals even without growth factors, driving uncontrolled cell division
It blocks all cell division permanently

quiz_questions.explanation: The Ras-MAPK pathway normally relays growth factor signals from cell surface to nucleus. When Ras is permanently active (stuck 'ON'), it continuously fires growth signals regardless of external growth factors. Each cascade step amplifies the signal, making a single stuck-ON Ras especially potent in driving cancer.
